summary: Hannes Mühleisen presents how DuckDB can be deployed at every tier of a data architecture, replacing the traditional centralized database server model. He argues that DuckDB's in-process, embeddable design allows it to run inside operational servers (via PG-DuckDB), in serverless Lambdas replacing Spark clusters, within app servers eliminating database protocol overhead, and even in browsers via WebAssembly. He frames this as a paradigm shift: state-of-the-art query processing is no longer bound to a fixed server or cluster but can live anywhere creativity allows.
key_points: DuckDB's in-process architecture was chosen deliberately for ease of use—no servers, no Docker, no DBAs, just a pip install
Despite its lightweight deployment, DuckDB is batteries-included with transactions, persistence, complex SQL, cloud storage access, and format support
Building a production-quality query engine is prohibitively expensive even for the richest organizations; DuckDB provides state-of-the-art for free under MIT
DuckDB can replace Spark clusters with serverless Lambdas due to millisecond startup time, tiny binary size, and far higher single-node efficiency
The paradigm shift is that the query engine is unshackled from centralized servers and can run anywhere—browsers, phones, cars, satellites
money_quotes: People generally fear data. It's my observation, like, especially when it stops fitting in Excel.
We have built a system to write papers maybe, or to look good in benchmarks, but we have not necessarily built systems to spark joy.
Building this kind of technology, like a query engine is actually not possible for even the richest organization.
Blind parallelization alone isn't good enough. You can parallelize a slow Java implementation across a thousand cores and it won't matter because it's still slow single core.

DuckDB can be everywhere in your stack, same SQL, same storage format, same capabilities. It's a state of the art processing engine that's now kind of unshackled from database servers. It's actually a massive paradigm shift.
Instead the database living on a centralized server or cluster, it can now live everywhere. It's only your creativity limits where you can put it.
We typically outperform Spark like a factor 10 or so on a single node. And Spark takes a lot of parallel cluster nodes to catch up.
You don't have to wait for VMs to go up or a cluster to be of the right size. You just run a Lambda.
key_insight: DuckDB's in-process architecture represents a paradigm shift where state-of-the-art analytical query processing is unshackled from centralized servers and can be embedded at every tier of a data architecture, from operational databases to browsers.
